name: Aragonese Way Trail
name_original: Puente la Reina/Gares a Obanos – Ruta Circular
description: Cycle an easy 9.8-mile (15.8 km) loop on the Aragonese Way Trail, passing historic bridges and monuments near Puente la Reina.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Puente La Reina/Gares are situated in the Valdizarbe Valley, where the Arga and Robo Rivers converge. The landscape features rolling hills, extensive vineyards, and olive groves, particularly in Central Navarre. The area is also characterized by its position south of the Sierra del Perdón, including the Alto del Perdón. These natural features provide varied terrain for touring cyclists. Best touring cycling routes around Puente La Reina/Gares The most popular touring cycling route is Aragonese Way Trail, a 9.8 miles (15.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 7 minutes to complete. This easy route follows a historic section of the Camino de Santiago. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Puente la Reina Romanesque Bridge – Estella-Lizarra loop from Puente la Reina / Gares, a difficult 28.2 miles (45.4 km) path. This route offers views of the Romanesque Bridge and connects to the town of Estella-Lizarra. Local touring cyclists also love the Romanesque Bridge (La Reina) – Canal de Navarra loop from Puente la Reina / Gares, a 20.2 miles (32.5 km) trail leading through the historic Romanesque Bridge area and alongside the Canal de Navarra, often completed in about 2 hours 11 minutes. Touring cycling around Puente La Reina/Gares is defined by rolling hills, scenic vineyards, and paths along the Arga River. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Puente La Reina/Gares are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 140 reviews. More than 1,200 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Puente La Reina/Gares's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Puente La Reina/Gares?
A: There are over 80 touring cycling routes around Puente La Reina/Gares, offering a wide range of options for cyclists. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 140 reviews.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes around Puente La Reina/Gares?
A: The terrain around Puente La Reina/Gares is characterized by rolling hills, scenic vineyards, and olive groves, particularly in Central Navarre. Routes often follow paths along the Arga River and traverse the Valdizarbe Valley. You'll also find sections near the Sierra del Perdón, offering varied landscapes.

Q: Are there routes suitable for beginners or less experienced touring cyclists?
A: Yes, there are routes suitable for various ability levels. For an easy ride, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e968199238/aragonese-way-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aragonese Way Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which is 9.8 miles (15.8 km) long and follows a historic section of the Camino de Santiago. There are 2 easy routes and 33 moderate routes available, alongside more challenging options.

Q: What historical landmarks or natural features can I see along the cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer views of significant landmarks. You can cycle past the iconic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/119191" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente la Reina Romanesque Bridge&lt;/a&gt;, the town's namesake. Other notable sights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/654847" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pilgrim Monument at Alto del Perdón&lt;/a&gt;, and the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1019580" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Bridge and Road of Andelos&lt;/a&gt;. The region's medieval architecture and the Valdizarbe Valley's vineyards also provide scenic backdrops.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, several circular routes allow you to start and end in the same location. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1286298" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Romanesque Bridge (La Reina) – Canal de Navarra loop from Puente la Reina / Gares&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 20.2 miles (32.5 km) trail. Another is the more challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24647999" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente la Reina Romanesque Bridge – Estella-Lizarra loop from Puente la Reina / Gares&lt;/a&gt;, covering 28.2 miles (45.4 km).

Q: Can I cycle a section of the Camino de Santiago near Puente La Reina/Gares?
A: Absolutely. Puente La Reina/Gares is a significant convergence point for the Aragonese and Navarrese branches of the Camino de Santiago. Many touring cycling routes incorporate sections of this historic pilgrimage path, such as the easy &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e968199238/aragonese-way-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aragonese Way Trail&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What makes Puente La Reina/Gares a notable destination for touring cyclists?
A: Puente La Reina/Gares is renowned for its strategic location on the Camino de Santiago, its rich medieval history, and diverse natural landscapes. It's also a stage on both EuroVelo 1 and EuroVelo 3, making it a well-connected and established cycling hub with varied terrain from river valleys to rolling hills and vineyards.

Q: Are there any challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, for experienced riders seeking a challenge, there are 47 difficult routes available. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40337653" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of the fields – Puente la Reina Romanesque Bridge loop from Legarda&lt;/a&gt;, a 26.5 miles (42.7 km) route with significant elevation changes.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Puente La Reina/Gares?
A: The region generally offers pleasant cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn are often ideal, with milder temperatures and vibrant landscapes. Summer can be warm, but the routes along the Arga River or through shaded vineyards can provide some relief. Winter cycling is possible, though cooler and potentially wetter.

Q: Are there any routes that offer panoramic views?
A: Many routes in the area provide expansive views, particularly those that ascend into the rolling hills or near the Sierra del Perdón. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3690724" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View from Alto del Perdón&lt;/a&gt; is a well-known viewpoint, often incorporated into longer rides, offering wide vistas of the Navarre countryside.

Q: Can I find routes that pass through vineyards or olive groves?
A: Yes, the region is known for its agricultural landscapes. Many touring cycling routes wind through scenic vineyards and olive groves, especially in Central Navarre, providing a rich sensory experience as you ride through the Valdizarbe Valley.

Q: What is the average rating for touring cycling routes in Puente La Reina/Gares?
A: The touring cycling routes in Puente La Reina/Gares are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an impressive average rating of 4.4 stars from over 140 reviews. This indicates a high level of satisfaction among cyclists who have explored the area.
tips: The statue of a pilgrim located at the entrance of the town of Puente la Reina. The monument, which measures more than two meters and weighs about 500 kilos, was inaugurated on August 29, 1965.
The church of San Juan Bautista was built at the beginning of the 20th century and is in the neo-Gothic style. It is located in the beautiful Plaza de los Fueros, in Obanos.

Numerous churches and shrines were built along the Camino de Santiago. Undoubtedly one of the most beautiful and emblematic is the Church of Santa María de Eunate, a true jewel of Romanesque art that, since it was built in 1170, stands alone on land belonging to the Villa de Muruzábal (Valdizarbe), in the Aragonese route of the Camino de Santiago entering Navarra from Somport through Sangüesa to meet very close to Eunate, in Puente la Reina, with that of Valcarlos. The place where it is located is solitary, flat and open, which enhances the spirituality and charm of the monument, something that, together with its artistic importance, makes it one of the most interesting Romanesque examples on the Camino de Santiago.
https://turismodenavarra.com/en/santa-maria-de-eunate/

Puente la Reina (Basque Gares) is a municipality with 2840 inhabitants. In Puente la Reina, the Aragonese and Navarrese branches of the Way of St. James meet, the common path crossing the Arga river via the bridge of the same name.

The name and development are linked to the bridge donated by Dona Mayor, Queen of Navarre, wife of King Sancho Mayor, or her daughter-in-law. Despite all the uncertainty regarding the founder, the construction of the bridge in the first half of the 11th century is certain.

Because river crossings were dangerous, detours were long and ferry services expensive, the streams of pilgrims soon concentrated on this bridge. This was followed by the settlement of Franks and the founding of a market town. The development of the city is an example of the infrastructure projects of this time and their effect, and can often be found in a comparable form on the Way of St. James.

In 1122 it received city rights, from 1235 the construction of the city wall began. García IV donated the city to the Templars, and after their dissolution at the beginning of the 14th century, it passed to the Hospitallers.

faqs.question: How challenging is the Aragonese Way Trail for touring cyclists?
faqs.answer: The Aragonese Way Trail is considered quite demanding for touring cyclists, especially in its early stages. It begins at Somport Pass in the Pyrenees at 1,632 meters, featuring a significant initial descent of over 400 meters in 7 km to Canfranc Estación. This section is steep and requires careful navigation. The route generally involves mountain passes and varied terrain, making it a physically rewarding experience.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the Aragonese Way Trail?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter diverse terrain, starting with challenging mountain passes in the Pyrenees. The route then follows the Aragón river valley, transitioning to quiet single-lane tracks, military roads, and forested slopes. You'll also cycle through open fields and parts of the impressive Foz de Lumbier nature reserve, known for its dramatic gorges.

faqs.question: What are the main sights and natural features along the Aragonese Way Trail?
faqs.answer: The trail offers stunning scenery, from the spectacular Pyrenees to the tranquil Aragón river valley. You'll pass medieval fortresses, Romanesque architecture, and traditional villages. Notable highlights include the Pilgrim Monument at Puente la Reina, the Church of Saint John the Baptist, Obanos, Santa María de Eunate, and the Puente la Reina Romanesque Bridge. The Foz de Lumbier nature reserve also offers dramatic gorges.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Aragonese Way Trail?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's mountainous sections in the Pyrenees, the best time to cycle is typically from late spring to early autumn (May to October). This avoids the snow and harsher conditions of winter, and the extreme heat of mid-summer in some lower-lying areas. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery.

faqs.question: Where does the Aragonese Way Trail begin and what are the access points?
faqs.answer: The Aragonese Way Trail officially begins at Somport Pass (Col du Somport) in the Pyrenees. This is the primary starting point for cyclists. Parking is generally available at or near major towns and villages along the route, such as Canfranc Estación or Puente la Reina, though specific trailhead parking can vary. Public transport options to Somport Pass might be limited, so planning your logistics for the start is crucial.

faqs.question: Is the Aragonese Way Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While there are no explicit restrictions mentioned, generally, dogs are allowed on the Aragonese Way Trail. However, it's essential to keep your dog on a leash, especially in populated areas, near livestock, or in nature reserves like Foz de Lumbier. Always carry water for your dog and be prepared to clean up after them. Accommodation options might be more limited when traveling with a dog, so plan ahead.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay entrance fees to cycle the Aragonese Way Trail?
faqs.answer: No, you do not typically need a permit or have to pay entrance fees to cycle the Aragonese Way Trail. It is a public pilgrimage route. However, if you plan to stay in specific accommodations or visit certain attractions along the way, those may have their own costs.

faqs.question: Does the Aragonese Way Trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Aragonese Way Trail is a branch of the Camino de Santiago. It ultimately merges with the more famous Camino Francés at Puente la Reina. The route also passes through sections of other official trails such as the Aragonese Way (63%), Camiño Francés (Rutas alternativas) (44%), and Camino Francés - 01 (36%).

faqs.question: What makes the Aragonese Way Trail a unique cycling experience?
faqs.answer: The Aragonese Way stands out for its tranquility and historical significance. It's described as one of the most solitary Jacobean routes, offering a peaceful alternative to the often-crowded Camino Francés. This allows for a deeper immersion in the beautiful landscapes, medieval heritage, and traditional villages without the constant presence of large numbers of pilgrims. It provides a unique blend of physical challenge, stunning natural beauty, and a quiet, intimate journey.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to cycle the entire Aragonese Way Trail?
faqs.answer: The entire Aragonese Way Trail, spanning approximately 165 to 172 kilometers, can typically be completed by bike in three stages by an experienced touring cyclist. The duration can vary depending on your fitness level, the amount of sightseeing you do, and the daily distances you plan to cover.

faqs.question: Are there many accommodation options along the Aragonese Way Trail?
faqs.answer: Compared to busier Camino routes, the Aragonese Way has fewer accommodation options due to its less-traveled nature. However, those available are often described as exceptional, contributing to a sense of adventuring into the 'unknown'. It's advisable to plan and book your accommodations in advance, especially during peak season, to ensure availability.

waypoints.name: Pilgrim Monument at Puente la Reina
Church of Saint John the Baptist, Obanos
